data.title: Civeo Corporation: Building Up To A 300% Upside
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome The Buy Side as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

As a full-time a long-term value investor searching for attractive companies for over a decade, it’s always nice to dive deep into predictable free cash-flow producing companies with a strong moat that are trading at a low price. With that in mind we look at Civeo Corporation (NYSE:CVEO) and walk through its market position, risks, current valuation based on their cash flows, shareholder structure and the expected shareholder value return. Given its leadership role in the modular structure industry, strong cash flow and tailwinds from stock buybacks planned, this value gem is a diamond in the rough.

Market Position

Civeo Corporation is an established leader in delivering temporary industry housing, and will undoubtedly be relevant in the long term considering its operations and market trends. Officially established in 2014 as a spin-off from Oil States International, the company began trading independently on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol CVEO on June 2, 2014, with its headquarters in Houston, Texas.

However, Civeo’s roots go back much further, as it brings together the experience of two predecessor companies:

  • In Canada, the story began in 1977 with Peace Trailer Industries (PTI), which initially rented mobile offices and accommodations for oil workers and later became one of the largest providers of workforce accommodations in North America.
  • In Australia, The MAC (ASX:MSL) was founded in 1996 and grew to become a leading provider of accommodations for workers in the resources sector.

Nowadays, Civeo Corporation specializes in providing workforce accommodations and hospitality services for the natural resource companies that are involved in oil, gas, coal, LNG, and iron ore production. Its core offerings include lodging, catering, housekeeping, facility management, and a range of support services. Civeo operates in key resource regions across Australia and Canada, providing long-term service agreements to deliver integrated solutions tailored to the needs of remote industrial operations.

Due to a recent acquisition of another four villages in the Australian Bowen basin, Civeo owns and operates a total of 28 lodges and villages in Australia and North America with an aggregate of approximately 27,500 rooms. In addition, Civeo operates and provides hospitality services at 22 customer-owned locations with more than 18,000 rooms.

The locations of their Australian villages are the following:

Meanwhile, their Canadian lodges are located here:

Interesting is their exposure to the full customer project life cycle. We can see here, that Civeo can cover the full project cycle of the customer and therefore generate high-quality recurring revenue.

It shows that revenues and predictable and stable. This is also reflected in their resilient revenue history over time:

Also, the well-capitalized customer base of Civeo is a positive thing to mention.

To get a quick picture of what one of these Civeo Villages looks like, here is one in Australia:

Outlook

The market outlook for hospitality service providers in the natural resources sector is cautiously optimistic for 2025. While commodity-driven demand is expected to remain steady, growth will be moderate, and operators must navigate cost pressures and heightened sustainability requirements.

For the Canadian market, Civeo is expecting headwinds due to macroeconomics and plans to reduce its workforce by around 25%. While this incurs a $1 million restructuring charge, the cost savings on labor are significant and would help Civeo weather the macroeconomic challenges its clients face.

On the other hand, is the Australian market further capitalizing on favorable market dynamics so that there will be strong occupancy levels and customer activity throughout their villages.

Therefore, management provides guidance for the full year 2025 to an expected revenue of $620-$650 million and $75-$85 million EBITDA.

Valuation

As value investors we want to buy great businesses for a lower price than what they’re worth. To calculate what a business is worth we have to take a look at its ability to generate free cash flow and put that into perspective with the price we must pay for these cash flows.

As Warren Buffett mentioned previously, “If you attempt to assess intrinsic value, it all relates to cash flows. The only reason for putting cash into any kind of investment now is because you expect to take cash out.”

Here he also refers to cash that is available for share repurchases, not only for dividends. Therefore, we can say that the most important thing to look at is free cash flow (FCF) available for repurchases or dividends. But there is one exception that should be adjusted for; normally FCF excludes the equity-based compensation since it's a non-cash expense. But for my valuation I treat equity-based compensation like the expense it really is for shareholders, due to its effect of share dilution.

Let’s take a closer look at Civeo's financials:

Here we see Civeo's strong ability to generate consistent free cash flow.

On average, Civeo was able to generate roughly $60 million annually over the last three years, which would be available for share buybacks, dividends, or repaying debt. This shows the company's resilience.

To put this into perspective with a share price, I calculate Civeo's Enterprise Value (EV) here.

I chose Enterprise Value because it is a particularly fitting way to determine this company's worth because it accounts for cash and debt, adding the net debt to the market cap. We obtain market cap by multiplying the total number of Civeo's outstanding shares by its current price per share.

Civeo's market cap: $20.92 x 13.453.260 shares outstanding = $281 million.

A market cap calculation completely excludes the debt the company still owes, which would reduce its ability to use free cash flow for shareholder return but rather have to devote more of it to service obligations.

Hence, we add the net debt position to the market cap to get a clearer picture of what the true price of the company is.

Net Debt = Total Debt − Cash and Cash Equivalents

Here we see the evolution of Civeo’s net debt over the last couple of years:

As we can see, because of its consistent ability to generate free cash flow, the net debt shrank from $351 million in 2016 to only $69 million as of the end of March 2025. That is quite impressive.

The market cap of $281 million and net debt of $69 million result in the current EV of $350 million.

But now let’s combine all that and calculate other metrics:

EV/FCF Is Key

Our most important metric here is EV/FCF, which stands at 5.9x currently. If inverted to 1/5.9, it implies a 17% total FCF return rate. As Charlie Munger said, "Invert, always invert."

Also let’s look at the net debt/FCF multiple of only 1.2x. This is important because we want to own companies that don’t owe a lot of debt. And particularly for Civeo that means they could pay off all their net debt with the FCF produced within around one year. That’s great. Following Warren Buffett's advice, I'm wary of companies with lots of long-term debt as it's sensitive to interest rate hikes and can significantly impact the predictability of cash flows and profits.

With the recent full year 2025 revenue and EBITDA guidance of $620 million to $650 million revenue and $75 million to $85 million EBITDA, these FCF levels are assumably sustainable for the foreseeable future.

Shareholder Structure

It's worth noting the shareholder structure here, as we get a sense of the ownership groups and interests that comes with it. First, we can say that institutional ownership interest is continuously growing in this company as we see in the following:

A more detailed look into the institutional ownership structure tells us that activist Investor Engine Capital took a large position in Civeo which accounts for about 9.8% of all outstanding shares.

Engine Capital sent a public letter to the board of directors, asking them to reevaluate the use of FCF due to its underperformance in share price and the opportunity to allocate this capital in a more shareholder-rewarding way via buybacks.

Additionally, management owns significant portions of the company, which is good and shows they are aligned with shareholder interests. Some of the largest shareholders are as follows:

Shareholder Return

There are two major ways of rewarding shareholders: Dividends and share buybacks. I'll examine them both.

Dividends

One way of returning capital to owners is through dividends. Civeo has not always distributed dividends, starting to do so just recently in 2023.

Personally, I think dividends can definitely be a way of returning capital to shareholders, but these must be cautiously evaluated, considering their sustainability and other uses of cash flow.

Share buybacks

Civeo also has a history of using FCF for buying back shares as we see:

I like seeing buybacks if the share price is low, because of the long-term value it creates.

We can't be certain about attributing the board's and management's recent decisions regarding dividends and buybacks to the letter from Engine Capital to the board of directors, or whether other reasons also had an impact. But we can say that Civeo announced in their Q1 earnings release that it will increase the repurchase authorization from 10% to 20% of outstanding shares, and after completion, to use 75% of FCF for buybacks, which is a great sign of commitment to shareholder return.

Fair value of Civeo

Given the recent Enterprise Value of $350 million and EV/FCF multiple of only 5.9x, Civeo definitely has room for price appreciation. So let's take a look at industry multiples:

We can see that the average EV/FCF multiple stands at 24.1x. If we compare that with Civeo's current multiple of 5.9x, we get a potential upside of 308%. That translates to a share price of about $65, give or take a few percent of that upside.

Risks and Uncertainties

To get a well-balanced overview of a company, we also must look at the risks and uncertainties that come with the business.

Commodity Price Exposure

Civeo’s top line performance is closely linked to the natural resources sector, particularly the oil and steel industry. This industry encompasses Civeo's main client concentration. Changes in commodity prices have a direct impact on client activity and capital investment, which influences the demand for Civeo’s workforce accommodations. This reliance on commodity prices creates a cyclical business model and therefore results in some revenue fluctuations. Civeo has no direct control over exposure to this risk.

Regulatory Framework

Civeo is operating in a complex regulatory framework itself stating that due to its operations in Canada, Australia, and the US, it has to comply with plenty of different regulators and environments. These regulations can change from time to time and are costly to comply with, which can therefore lead to unexpected expenses.

Competitive Pressure

Civeo operates in a competitive industry shaped by several critical factors. The competitive environment, like Fleetwood Corporation in Australia and can result in lower occupancy rates and pricing pressure, which may negatively impact Civeo's revenue and profitability. While Civeo is the dominant market player amongst its competitors in Australia, it faces competition mainly from Fleetwood Corporation, which offers temporary housing that overlaps with Civeo's product offering to mining industry and infrastructure clients. In Canada, competitors are mainly ATCO Structures and Logistics (ATC.X), Black Diamond Group Limited (BDI:CA), and Dexterra Group Inc. (DXT:CA).

Civeo maintains market dominance because its size and scale allow it to serve its target industry clients efficiently. It's able to lock in long-term revenue streams through the use of long-term contracts. Also, Civeo's value-add above its competition is in its comprehensive suite of services like facilities maintenance and even catering. Therefore, a risk to consider is if clients become more cost-sensitive and prefer a more à la carte approach, putting at risk both the long-duration nature of contracts and the cost of add-ons related to a full-service package.

Conclusion

Civeo Corporation is a solid player with a bright outlook, manageable risks, a management team that is aligned with shareholder interests, strong FCF, and a share repurchase plan in place, that will benefit shareholders long-term. I value this company right now as a long-term portfolio addition due to its unique position and economics. Given the difference between my valuation and its trading price now, I rate it as a Buy.

data.title: INmune Bio: XPro Trial Readout In Alzheimer's Disease Offers Big Opportunity
data.description:
Background

INmune Bio, Inc. (NASDAQ:INMB) announced Q1 2025 business and financial results on May 8th, 2025, beating the analyst’s estimates. More importantly, the company claims to be on track for sharing results of the MINDFul Phase II clinical trial in the second half of June.

The MINDFul trial is evaluating the safety and efficacy of XPro in patients with early Alzheimer’s disease (AD). XPro is a brain-penetrant, highly selective inhibitor of the soluble form of the Tumor Necrosis Factor ((sTNF)), a cytokine produced by activated microglial cells that is associated with the increased neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration observed in AD.

Unlike Leqembi (lecanemab) developed by Esai Co. (OTCPK:ESAIY) and Biogen Inc. (BIIB), and Kisunla (donanemab) from Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY), XPro’s mechanism of action has the potential to provide a safe therapeutic solution to patients with early AD, regardless of apolipoprotein E ε4 ((ApoE ε4) expression, and without increasing their risk of developing amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA). ARIA is a sign of swelling and/or bleeding of the brain, which is one of the major risk factors associated with the anti-amyloid treatments (e.g., Leqembi and Kisunla).

Interestingly, Tiziana Life Sciences Ltd. (TLSA), which is also targeting neuroinflammation, announced that foralumab induced a significant reduction in microglia activation in a patient with moderate AD (see image below). Suggesting its therapeutic potential in that patient group and supporting the scientific rationale of XPro’s mechanism of action.

Thesis

The Alzheimer’s disease global market is expected to grow at a 23% CAGR between 2023 and 2033, reaching up to $19.3 billion by 2033. Despite the large market size, and the highly unmet clinical need, early AD remains difficult to accurately diagnose and treat. Indeed, Leqembi and Kisunla have struggled to convince some regulatory agencies about their therapeutic advantage in light of the associated risk of developing ARIA. Resulting in labels limiting its use to patients with early AD who are non-carriers or carry only one copy (i.e., heterozygotes) of the ApoE ε4 gene. In this sense, INmune Bio estimates that XPro patient opportunity accounts for at least 70% of patients with early AD:

Based on the data INmune Bio and other companies presented at ADPD, we now believe more than two-thirds of early Alzheimer’s disease patients will be eligible for XPro based on ApoE4 status alone. I remind you that ApoE4 positivity was one of the four enrichment or enrollment criteria we used in the MINDFuL study.

The ApoE4 status of patients in the MINDFuL is almost identical to what is reported in recent major trials in Alzheimer’s. Patients with at least one ApoE4 allele make up more than two-thirds of the patients in these trials. This means the market opportunity for XPro in early AD has increased to nearly 70% of early AD patients, not the 40% we have previously been talking about.

Considering the highly unmet clinical need in early AD and the AD projected market size, I would expect that positive Phase II clinical trial results are likely to have a positive impact on INMB's share price, as well as raise interest from suitable acquirers/partners. Particularly if the MINDFul provides strong safety and therapeutic performance in early AD. Conversely, if XPro underperforms, INmune Bio might struggle to fund operations beyond its cash runway, Q3 2025.

INmune Bio is also developing CORDStrom, for the treatment of epidermolysis bullosa (EB). CORDStrom’s regulatory filings in the USA, Europe, and the U.K. are scheduled for 2026 (see image below). EB’s global market size is projected to reach $9.9 billion by 2032, with an 11.2% CAGR from 2025 to 2032. Nonetheless, given the short cash runway, I believe XPro’s clinical performance is going to be key to successfully funding operations beyond Q3 2025. Thus, this time around, I will not dive into CORDStrom or INKmune potential.

Based on XPro’s good scientific rationale, previous clinical performance, and Tiziana’s clinical results on microglia activation, XPro has the potential to deliver strong Phase II clinical results by mid-June, while CORDStrom may become commercially available by 2027, if successful at regulatory filings. Hence, I consider INMB a high-risk/high-reward speculative buy, supporting my “Strong Buy” rating for investors with high-risk tolerance.

Alzheimer’s Disease

Alzheimer’s disease is a degenerative neurological condition affecting the central nervous system, which impairs cognitive performance and ultimately can cause dementia in people with AD.

AD was first reported by Alois Alzheimer, a clinical psychiatrist and neuroanatomist in 1906. However, it was only by January 2023 when the first treatment aimed to slow the progression of AD, Leqembi, obtained its first regulatory approval in the U.S. Leqembi was later followed by Kisunla and obtained FDA approval in July 2024.

AD is usually divided by stages, including preclinical, mild cognitive impairment, mild dementia, moderate dementia, and severe dementia due to AD. The table below illustrates the different stages of AD, the symptoms, and associated biomarkers. Note the stages listed as mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and mild dementia due to AD are usually grouped as “early AD.”

To date, no treatment has been demonstrated to reverse the effects of AD, highlighting the importance of early diagnosis and supporting the need for novel treatments that target the root of the disorder and even restore the cognitive performance of people with AD.

In terms of causes, AD’s initiation and progression are usually associated with the formation and accumulation of amyloid-β ((Aβ)) plaques in the brain, which impairs neuronal communication and are the target of Leqembi and Kisunla. A second hallmark of AD is the accumulation of phosphorylated Tau, also known as pathological Tau ((P-Tau)).

Tau is an intracellular filament required for maintaining the structure and synaptic function of neurons. On the other hand, P-Tau is a damaged version of Tau, which forms intracellular bundles called “neurofibrillary tangles,” impeding the normal function and communication between neurons. P-Tau accumulation is highly correlated with the cognitive impairment observed in people with AD. P-Tau 217, in particular, can be identified and quantified from blood samples, and it is a very accurate marker for diagnosis and assessing the progression of AD. Note other companies, including Voyager Therapeutics (VYGR), are developing anti-AD therapies targeting P-Tau; check my latest coverage on VYGR and its competitive landscape here.

Neuroinflammation is the third hallmark of AD, which is present from the early stages of the disease, i.e., preclinical. Until a few years ago, researchers believed that neuroinflammation was only the consequence of Aβ plaques and P-Tau accumulation, but the most recent studies revealed the critical role of activated microglial cells and astrocytes in AD and neuroinflammation.

Although it remains unclear if neuroinflammation is one of the causes of AD, it is clear that neuroinflammation is associated with the spread of the P-Tau and Aβ plaques, becoming a significant driver of the disease and the link with the other two disease hallmarks.

Microglial cells, in particular, are considered a double-edged sword. They are the CNS immune cells, being in a resting state under standard physiological conditions while still monitoring all activity, supporting the healthy function of neurons and other CNS residents, and shifting into an activated state when stimulated.

The activated microglial cells and astrocytes are key initiators of neuroinflammation in AD and other neurodegenerative conditions. Activated microglial cells release inflammatory cytokines, which in turn attract and activate more astrocytes and microglial cells. This activity is not necessarily bad when temporarily activated microglial cells eliminate Aβ aggregates via phagocytosis. However, the continuous activation of microglial cells leads to sustained release of inflammatory cytokines, including sTNF, the reduction of the microglial cells capacity to eliminate Aβ and neurotoxins, the weakening of the brain-blood barrier that protects the CNS, the propagation of P-Tau and Aβ plaques, the disruption of the synaptic activity and death of neurons, and ultimately AD progression. Thus, highlighting the rationale behind tackling neuroinflammation and microglial activation as a therapeutic strategy against AD progression.

XPro

XPro 1595 is a brain-penetrant highly specific sTNF inhibitor, which is administered via subcutaneous injection. INmune acquired XPro’s global rights for development and commercialization from Xencor Inc. (XNCR) in 2017. XPro’s mechanism of action, see image below, enables it to neutralize sTNF by forming non-functional heterodimers while sparing transmembrane TNF ((tmTNF)).

This specificity is key to understanding the potential therapeutic advantage of XPro against AD. Soluble TNF, in the context of AD, can be described as the pro-inflammatory signal driving neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration, being responsible for shifting microglial activation from a regenerative to a pro-inflammatory state, inhibiting axon remyelination, inflammation and cell death. Meanwhile, tmTNF has the opposite effects, driving neuroprotection, healthy CNS immune activity, improves synaptic plasticity and remyelination of axons (see image below). In other words, XPro has the potential to restore the CNS immune response, blocking the exacerbated neuroinflammatory response commonly observed in patients with AD.

Building on this, in 2021, XPro’s Phase I clinical trial results demonstrated its capacity to reduce neurodegenerative and neuroinflammation markers in people with mild to moderate AD after 12 weeks of once-weekly subcutaneous injections. A posteriori analysis of samples obtained from the Phase I trial participants published last year demonstrated that XPro promotes the expression of proteins involved in axon regeneration, remyelination, and synapses.

Despite having the support of good scientific rationale and early clinical trial performance, the Phase II trial results are going to be key to understanding the therapeutic effect of XPro in patients with early AD. The MINDFul Phase II trial is expected to yield top-line results by mid-June. INMB’s management is very optimistic about XPro’s potential to demonstrate disease-modifying effects. Although so far the data is blinded, during the last earnings call the company reported on the safety profile in all cohorts:

On the call six weeks ago, I also mentioned the safety profile of XPro in the MINDFuL trial. Nothing has changed. There are no reports of ARIA. No patients have had unscheduled MRIs due to pNF symptoms or headache, etc., and there have been no deaths. So far, XPro is safe and well-tolerated in this patient population that has an average age of 73 years old, and many of them have a long list of comorbidities.

If confirmed, the safety profile will be advantageous in terms of XPro’s population opportunity, but without a doubt the most important outcome of this study will be related to XPro’s impact on cognition. In this sense, the company is putting significant effort into building and validating a novel scale system specifically designed for objectively quantifying the cognitive performance of people with early AD. This scale is EMACC; if you haven’t yet, I recommend this video, where they explained the EMACC in full. Briefly, the EMACC was specifically designed for assessing the disease progression or improvements in people with early AD, providing higher sensitivity and precision than the other scales that were designed for other stages of the disease (see image below).

Considering the objectivity and sensitivity of the EMACC (see image below), I believe that the scale will be able to discern any positive or negative changes in the cognitive performance of the study participants.

However, the FDA and other regulatory agencies are yet to accept or dismiss data based on the EMACC. In the case of being dismissed, the company would be forced to stick with CDR, which has a lower sensitivity to discern changes in early AD, as the primary endpoint in future studies.

Taking together XPro’s performance in preclinical studies and Phase I clinical trial, its mechanism of action reducing neuroinflammation and promoting neuroregeneration, I believe XPro is likely to meet the primary endpoint, showing significant therapeutic efficacy when measured with EMACC (see image below).

Building on the speculation of good Phase II performance, the company will be seeking to start Phase III trials as soon as the FDA clears the study design. Leading me to highlight the company’s short cash runway and financials in the next section.

Q1 2025 Financials

INmune reported Q1 2025 earnings last week. INMB is a clinical stage company; as such, it lacks significant product revenues, while the operating expenses are mostly associated with the cost of research & development. In this sense, the company reported a 257% total revenue increase in Q1 2025 when compared against the same period of 2024 (see image below), which was attributed to selling human mesenchymal stem cells ((hMSCs)) to third parties.

The operating expenses decreased 9.75% in Q1 2025, due to nearing the completion of the MINDFul Phase II clinical trial. Hence, contributing to an 11.6% net income increase and an EPS increase of 29% in Q1 2025 vs. Q1 2024. Finally, the company reported $19 million in cash and equivalents by the end of March 2025, which is 25.6% lower than the figures reported the year before.

I estimate INMB’s quarterly cash burn rate to be equivalent to the Q1 2025 operating expenses, $9.955 million. Then, considering the current cash and equivalent, the company’s cash runway is two quarters, which is in line with the management’s projections, i.e., having sufficient funds to cover operations until Q3 2025.

The short cash runway is unlikely to be a surprise for INMB’s investors. Nonetheless, it remarks on the importance of XPro’s clinical performance. Should XPro deliver strong clinical results, demonstrating safety and therapeutic efficacy outperforming Leqembi and Kisunla, INmune Bio is likely to have many suitable options to finance operations beyond Q3 2025. Among those, I would expect suitable partners and/or acquisition offers. On the other hand, should XPro deliver poor results, the company might struggle to find favorable funding opportunities, limiting their options to diluting shareholders and/or raising debt at unfavorable conditions.

Valuation

INMB shares are trading around $7.30, and have a $169 million market cap. The shares have observed significant volatility in the last week, amid the multiple headwinds and uncertainties negatively impacting the Healthcare sector, versus the positive impact of XPro’s Phase II results speculations. For instance, INMB observed a 15.6% surge between May 9th and May 13th, while the shares have fallen 9.6% in the last two days. However, the 47% share price surge year-to-date remarks INMB’s positive performance in the recent months.

The Wall Street analyst’s consensus is bullish on INMB, rating the shares with an overall Buy and a $23.4 average share price target in the next twelve months (i.e., 220% upside potential from the current price). Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating is in line with Wall Street, while the Seeking Alpha analyst’s consensus is rating the stock with a Strong Buy amid XPro and CORDStrom’s potential to succeed. Building on this, EPS projections are estimating an 18% EPS growth in 2025, a very slow growth in 2026 (1.46% 2026 EPS growth), while in 2027 and 2028 the EPS is expected to return to over 20% growth. Those projections are in line with the speculation of positive Phase II clinical results associated with XPro, as well as positive regulatory performance of CORDStrom, whose BLA submission is scheduled for H1 2026.

INmune’s pipeline performance, led by XPro, supports my speculative “Strong Buy” rating for high-risk tolerant investors. However, it is very important to highlight the high level of risk associated with such investment. Should XPro deliver unimpressive results, for instance, unclear clinical significance of XPro’s therapeutic capacity, the company is likely to struggle to fund operations beyond Q3 2025. Hence, interested investors should exercise caution and utilise risk management strategies.

Risks

Along this article, I have highlighted the short cash runway as one of the major risks to my bullish thesis. The Q3 2025 cash runway is the reason why I dismissed CORDStrom and INKmune’s potential in the current analysis. Should XPro deliver poor results, the other two product candidates will be almost irrelevant. Yet, the scientific rationale and XPro’s performance to date, support an optimistic outlook.

Should XPro beat expectations, not only INMB’s shares are likely to spike, but also INmune’s probabilities to receive very interesting offers from potential partners and/or acquirers that might result in high returns to investors.

Building on this speculation, once the company has secured funding, CORDStrom BLA submission, and INKmune’s performance in solid tumors will become very relevant for the company’s outlook. From the long-term outlook standpoint, INmune is likely to face strong pressures from other biopharmaceutical companies commercializing and/or developing products targeting AD. In this sense, should XPro yield the expected positive results, I would not be surprised if INmune gets acquired in the near future.

Beyond INmune’s specific risks, investors should be aware of the strong headwinds that the Pharma, Biotech and the overall Healthcare sector are facing in 2025. For instance, the numerous uncertainties associated with the changes in regulatory policies might negatively impact the development of INmune’s product candidates.

Conclusions

INmune Bio is expected to share the long-awaited results of the MINDFul by mid-June. This Phase II clinical trial is evaluating the safety, tolerance, and therapeutic efficacy of XPro in patients with early AD. CORDStrom and INKmune, are the other two product candidates in INmune’s pipeline. Both product candidates have shown promising results in clinical trials, with CORDStrom expected to file for regulatory approvals in H1 2026.

Despite CORDStrom’s and INKmune’s performance, my speculative “Strong Buy” rating is mainly supported on the expectation of XPro achieving the MINDFul primary and secondary endpoints, demonstrating strong therapeutic performance by significantly reducing neuroinflammation and improving cognition when compared against placebo.

Should XPro succeed in Phase II, demonstrating its therapeutic capacity and safety profile, then it would be plausible to expect the share price to surge, potentially reaching the Wall Street analyst average share price target, standing at $23.4. This speculation could support the speculation of attractive acquisition offers from larger biopharmaceutical companies and/or partnerships to co-develop XPro. Hence, securing funds that would extend the cash runway beyond Q3 2025, without diluting shareholders.

On the other hand, INmune is likely to struggle to fund operations beyond Q3 if XPro disappoints. Forcing the company to attempt to fund operations via shareholder dilutions and/or raising debt at unfavorable rates. In addition, the macroeconomic headwinds that the sector is facing this year might worsen the company’s chances of getting funding at favorable rates.

Overall, I believe XPro’s performance to date and scientific rationale support INmune Bio’s optimism about the upcoming Phase II clinical trial results and my “Strong Buy” rating for high-risk tolerant investors. Should investors consider increasing or starting positions in INMB, they should also consider the speculative nature of such investment based on potential clinical trial success.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Drops
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment increased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 6.5 percentage points to 35.9%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the 18th time in 20 weeks and is above 30% for only the sixth time this year.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 0.6 percentage points to 19.7%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 43rd time in 45 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 7.1 percentage points to 44.4%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 24th time in 26 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 13.6 percentage points to –8.5%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 19th time in 21 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members what they think about the Federal Reserve’s decision to keep interest rates unchanged.

Here is how they responded:

  • It was the right move: 73.6%
  • They should have cut rates: 17.1%
  • They should have raised rates: 2.7%
  • Not sure/no opinion: 6.6%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 35.9%, up 6.5 percentage points
  • Neutral: 19.7%, up 0.6 percentage points
  • Bearish: 44.4%, down 7.1 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.
